title: Nib angle and pen hold for the broad-edge nib
subtitle: Setting up the pen to produce thick-and-thin strokes
excerpt: The thick-and-thin contrast that makes broad-edge calligraphy distinctive comes entirely from the nib's angle to the baseline and how the pen is held. Set the nib correctly and the thick strokes write themselves. Get it wrong and the calligraphy looks smeared, ragged, or uniformly thin regardless of how slow or careful the strokes are.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), chapter on the broad-nib pen, pen-hold, and the pen angle. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/writingilluminat00john. Johnston's codification of the broad-edge nib's mechanics underlies all modern Foundational calligraphy instruction.

content.text: Broad-edge calligraphy is the art of a chisel-shaped nib held at a consistent angle. The
broad-edge nib
lays down strokes whose thickness is determined entirely by the relationship between the direction of movement and the
nib angle

to the baseline. A stroke drawn straight down while the nib edge is held at 30° to the baseline produces the maximum stroke width, the full width of the nib face. The same stroke at 90° produces a thin vertical line. This thick-and-thin relationship is not a skill the writer imposes: it is a geometric consequence of the nib geometry.
What you need

items.qty: Small amount in a low wide jar
items.name: Iron-gall or carbon-black ink
Cartridge paper, 130 gsm, practice sheet with ruled lines

items.qty: 1 sheet, for use as a hand-guard under the writing hand
items.name: Tracing paper (optional)

content.text: Fitting and priming the nib

New Mitchell Roundhand nibs have a lacquer coating to prevent corrosion in the packet. Remove it before the first use by passing the nib briefly through a flame (a match or lighter), blowing it out immediately, and wiping the nib face with a clean cloth. Alternatively, push the nib carefully into a raw potato for thirty seconds, the acid removes the coating.

Fit the nib into the pen holder with the ink reservoir (the small spring clip on Mitchell nibs) on the underside. The reservoir should sit against the flat face of the nib and just touch the nib tip without pushing it open. If the reservoir gap is too wide, the ink runs fast and floods; too narrow, and the nib does not release ink at all. Bend the reservoir gently with a fingernail if needed.
The pen hold

Hold the pen holder loosely between the index finger and middle finger, with the thumb supporting from the opposite side. The pen rests in the first joint of the index finger, not the fingertip. The hand rests on the side of the little finger or on a guard sheet of tracing paper to protect the writing surface from hand oils. The pen should point diagonally backward over the shoulder, not straight back, the natural angle for a relaxed arm position.
Setting the nib angle
For Foundational (Edward Johnston's hand), the
nib angle

is 30° from the baseline. This means the nib edge runs diagonally across the baseline, the left corner of the nib edge is higher than the right corner. At 30°, a straight downward stroke produces the thickest possible mark; a stroke from upper-left to lower-right at 45° to the baseline produces a thin stroke; and a pure horizontal stroke produces a medium-width mark. Vary the angle and the entire letter-weight logic changes.

Check the angle visually: dip the nib and make a single downward stroke on scrap paper. The left edge of the stroke should be roughly vertical; the right edge should lean slightly to the left. If the stroke is symmetrical (a pure rectangle), the nib is at 90°, rotate the hand clockwise until the stroke shows the characteristic slight lean.
Measuring x-height in nib widths
Before ruling guide lines, establish the
x-height

for the chosen nib and letter-proportions. Hold the nib horizontally (at 90° to the vertical) and make a series of small square marks stacked on top of each other. Each square is one nib-width. For Foundational lower-case, stack four nib-widths to establish the x-height line. The ascenders extend to 7 nib-widths; the descenders drop to 3 nib-widths below the baseline.
The move, first strokes
Load the nib by dipping to just below the reservoir opening, then touching the nib tip to a scrap sheet to release any excess ink. Excess ink floods the first stroke.
Place the nib on the paper at the x-height line. The entire flat edge of the nib should rest on the paper, both corners touching simultaneously.
Draw the stroke downward at a steady pace. Pull, do not push: broad-edge calligraphy uses pulling strokes (toward the body or the right). Pushing strokes catch the nib corners on rough paper.
Lift the nib cleanly at the end of each stroke, do not trail it off. The ink meniscus will break cleanly if the lift is quick and vertical.

attrs.intro: Problems with nib setup and pen hold.

items.fix: Touch the loaded nib to a scrap sheet before starting. The first touch deposits the excess. Narrow the reservoir gap slightly by pressing the spring clip with a fingernail.
items.cause: Too much ink in the reservoir, or the reservoir gap is too wide.
items.symptom: The ink floods on the first stroke and makes a large blot

items.fix: Lower the pen angle to the paper surface, the pen shaft should be at roughly 45° to the desk surface, not vertical. Both nib corners must contact the paper simultaneously.
items.cause: One corner of the nib is not touching the paper. Usually the right corner is lifting, from a nib held too upright relative to the paper surface.
items.symptom: Strokes are ragged on one edge but clean on the other

items.fix: Open the reservoir gap very slightly. If the coating was incompletely removed, pass the nib through a flame again and wipe clean.
items.cause: The reservoir gap is too narrow, preventing ink from reaching the nib tip. Or the nib coating was not fully removed.
items.symptom: The ink does not flow at all

attrs.heading: Common problems
